There are 24 circular shrines across the map, broken up into clearings. Clearings are groups of shrines dived by patches of dark forest

Around the edge of the board are pairs of colored dolmens (the stonehenge looking things)

All the bits:

  • Provisions (bags with numbers): main currency that we'll spend on most actions
  • Fire tiles (circular campfires)
  • Mistletoe (white berries)
  • Stone tablets (grey tiles arranged around central marker)
  • Magic potions (green rectangular tiles)

Central board with VP, knowledge track, gemstones, and everyone's unavailable dice

Everyone gets two playerboards:

One board has stuff we'll be putting out onto the board:

  • Runestones (big)
  • Standing stones (small)
  • Medicinal herbs on the sickle track

docs.title: Round overview
docs.text:
We'll take turns clockwise each taking a turn that is up to 7. Steps will always resolve in the same order, although all steps might not resolve as a few are checks

  • Move your druid
    • First turn you move into the adjacent shrine for free. On all other turns:
    • Move to a shrine not already containing one of your dice
    • Must move along path between shrines, there are some bridges (no 4 way intersections)
    • Pay provisions:
      • 1 per shrine entered or pass through
      • 1 per dark forest area crossed
  • Place a die
    • Place a die from your supply on a free space in that shrine
    • Pay provisions equal to the die value
  • Competition
    • If there are multiple dice on the shrine, the player who owns the highest value die gets 2 points
    • Ties are broken by the knowledge track
  • Action
    • Perform the action covered by your die
  • Fire
    • If you are the first player to have die in all shrines around a fire pit:
      • Gain the bonuses shown on the fire pit
      • Cover it with a fire tile. Now it is blocked
  • Dolmens
    • If your dice create a continuous path between two matching dolmens
      • Gain 2 points per die on the shortest connecting path
      • Take both of the dolmens off the board
      • Take a beige dolmen from the shared board and score the points shown (if still available)
  • Herbs
    • Gain herbs from the sickle track that you reached or passed this turn
    • Place them attached to your second playerboard, one face down, and one face up
      • Face up token provides a power or special action that is now always available to you
      • Face down is blocked and will never be used
    • All powers defined in the linked player aid

docs.title: Gain dice or provisions
docs.text:
• Gain 12 provisions or
  • Gain 3 of your player color dice from the shared board. Grab any dice, do not re-roll.

docs.title: Sickle track gain
docs.text:
• Go up indicated number on sickle track
  • Herbs aren't gained until last step in turn

docs.title: Knowledge gain
docs.text:
• Go up the indicated number on the knowledge track
  • Stack on top of any you land on

For any of those actions, if you have reached the end of the track (or run out of dice) and would gain the bonus again, just gain 2 points

docs.title: Gain tablet
docs.text:
• There is a tablet display with 12 rectangular tablet tiles
  • Grab any of the 12 faceup tablet tiles
  • Each tablet depicts an end game objective
  • Every completed objective on a tile you own is worth 10 points
  • Gain the bonus on the 2 smaller adacent oracle tiles next to the column you grabbed the tablet tile from
  • Replenish the empty spot from the draw stack
  • There are 2 of each tablet, you can double up (so meeting a condition once would satisfy both tablets)

docs.title: Amulet
docs.text:
• Adds gemstones to the amulet on your playerboard
  • The action icon will indicate a row (1,2, or 3) and an amulet multiplier
  • Take a gemstone from the shared board that you don't already have
  • Starting with the leftmost slot, put the gem in the indicated row of your amulet
  • Gain the bonus shown on the gemstone a number of times based on the multiplier
  • End of game get 10 pts per row completed
  • 20 points for completing left column

docs.title: Brewing potions
docs.text:
• Gain the number of mistletoe indicated
  • Brew up to the number of potions shown (cauldron icon)
    • To brew pick any available potion from the supply
    • Pay the mistletoe cost
    • Resolve immediately, and in your current location if it is a location specific action (like standing stones)
    • If brewing multiple potions, you can use mistletoe generated from one to pay for the other

docs.title: Place standing stones (small)
docs.text:
• Take an available standing stone from your board
  • Gain the reward shown
  • Add it to your location
  • Now gain the reward of every one of your deployed standing stones (including the one you just placed)

docs.title: Place a runestone (large)
docs.text:
• Chose any available runestone
  • Add it to your location
  • Gain the bonus shown for the runestone based on the pip value of the die you used to take the aciton
  • You will always get better rewards for higher die values
  • No other action cares about die value

docs.title: Druid in distress
docs.text:
If you can't afford to move and/or place a die (or if you choose to), you can take a distress turn

  • Ignore normal turn structure
  • Gain 6 provisions
  • Gain 1 die from the shared board (if able)

docs.title: Game end
docs.text:
Game end is triggered when one player has placed all of their dice

  • Once everyone has had the same number of turns, the game is over

docs.title: Scoring
docs.text:
• Add number of dice left holding, mistletoe, provisions. 6:1 for points
  • 10 pts per completed stone tablet
  • 10 pts per filled amulet row
  • 20 points if amulet left column full
  • Points from sickle space reached
  • On board shrine control
    • Each shrine is controlled by the person with the single highest die value
    • Ties are broken by the knowledge track
    • Remove each die except the highest value one from each space to make this easy
    • Each player counts up the total number of dice they have left on the board plus the number of runestones and standing stones in those shrines (total number of dice+stones)
    • Multiply this number by the furthest multiplyer you've reached on the knowledge track

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The planet is dying and we're migrating our societies to a new planet!

The game is played over 4 rounds

docs.title: Board overview
docs.text:
THe gross yellow planet is the old world where all of our existing stuff is, the other planet is the new world ripe for expansion.

  • Round track up top
  • Turn order in corner
  • Sattelite track twisting between both planets

  • Market of statdiums, spaceships, and

  • Playerboard

    • Center of the board is technologies
    • Section at the bottom of the board represents your actions
    • Your color player disks are population markers
    • Left side has a production track for the new world, with a marker for each resource

!!! 

Important concept: old world vs new world

The two sides of your playerboard represent the old world and the new world. Each of these has to have its components and production kept separate. Resources and cards built on the old world side need to be kept on the old world side, and vice versa. Spaceships can (and will) be used to transport things from the old world to the new world. As you get set up on the new world, more and more of your resources will be produced over on that side of your board.

If an effect tells you to gain something, you choose which world it comes in on

docs.title: Round overview
docs.text: Each round is made up of 7 phases

docs.title: Income
docs.text:
Produce resources on both worlds, feed and entertain populations

  • Each of your worlds produces
    • Old world
      • Each icon will produce the resource of it's color. At the start of the game, this is 7 of each resource
    • New world
      • Produce based on resource markers on production track. At the start of the game, this is 0 for everything
  • Feed populations
    • Refer to table near the round tracker on the main board
    • Shows how much food is needed for each world in each round
    • So in round 1, you need 5 food for the old, nothing for the new
    • Where in round 4, you'll need 1 food for old, and 4 for the new
    • If you can't pay the full cost, take 1 penalty chip for every food you can't pay
  • Entertainment
    • Refer to the table near the round tracker on the main board
    • Check if the new world has enough stadiums
    • At the start of the 3rd round you must have at least 1, start of the 4th round you must have 2, end of the game you must have 3
    • For each stadium you're missinga at one of these checks, take a penalty chip

docs.title: Actions
docs.text:
We will take turns taking a single action in turn order. There are ten icons at the bottom of your board that show the possible actions. The number under each action block represents its power

  • Choose an action to perform
  • Play a card
    • You can play a card face down under the block with the action you want to perform, and will then perform that action
    • You can also play a card to perform it's text effect instead of an action printed on the board
  • Advance your action marker one space on the track (the orange one above the card slots) and then pay any energy cost shown.
    • Your first two actions are free, with further actions costing you energy
    • You will pay an additional energy for playing the 4th (and further) card in a single power slot (reminder shown next to action track)
    • The power slot on the far right of the board can only have a single card played under it
  • Where energy paid for actions comes from depends on your markers position on the progress track (reminder in yellow box on halfway point of progress track)
    • If both of your markers are before the halfway point, then the action energy cost is paid from the old world
    • If you have one marker on either side of the halfway point, then energy can be paid from either or both worlds
    • If both markers are beyond halfway, energy cost is paid from the new world

docs.title: World agnostic actions
docs.text:
None of these actions are associated with a particular world

  • Gain and replace (left side of 4)
    • Gain one of any resource to either world (symbol is the three way box)
    • Up to three times you may exchange one resource for any other resource (on the same world)
    • You can peform these exchanges split up between the planets, but the exchanges are within each planet (can't remove from A to place on B)
  • Research a tech (right side of 4 and 1)
    • You have 3 columns of techs on your playerboard, with research happening in each column bottom to top
    • Taking the resource actions lets you place a marker on a tech
    • The level 1 techs require only 1 research to activate
    • Level 2 and 3 require two research actions. For the first step put your marker on the left, the second step move it to the right
    • If there is a lightning bolt, resolve the effect as soon as the tech is completed. The effect is the text, the icon up top just shows which action or phase the tech is associated with

docs.title: Either world actions
docs.text:
These actions require you spending resources to build things, which can be done on either of the two worlds

  • Build spaceship (middle of two)
    • At the start of this action, you can spend 1 resource from any planet to discard any number of ships from the lineup, slide remaining ships down, and replenish (max once)
    • Choose any ship from the market. Slide and replenish immediately
    • Pay the resource cost shown in the top left (cost must come totally from one world), then the ship is built into that world (so on that side of your board)
  • Build a stadium (right of two)
    • Works exactly the same way, including optional refresh
  • Build a factory (left of 3)
    • Spend a resource correspoinding to a factory tile that you still have
    • Build that type of factory onto the world where you paid the resources

The things built with these actions can all be built on either world, and can be transported between worlds

docs.title: New world actions
docs.text:
• Cloning (middle of 3)
    • Pay one food (green) to produce two population markers (player color disks) into the new world (on your board)
  • Settling (left of 2)
    • Choose an empty valid hex on the new world
    • To be valid, must meet these conditions:
      • Hex must be in a biome that you are allowed to settle in. Starting off you can only build in white. One one of your progress markers has advanced far enough the yellow symbol means you can build in desert, then the green symbol means you can build in forests, blue means water
      • If there are settlement icons at the top of your hex in the black banner (suns and hurricane swirls), then you must currently have those icons available based on the position of your markers on the progress track. Your marker must currently be in a space with that icon to be able to build in a space with that icon requirement.
      • You must have enough population and/or factories to completely settle the hex. Population markers go on circles, factories go on squares. So a spot with two cirlces and a square requires two population markers and one factory. Colors are not a requirement, they are a bonus.
    • Pay 1 steel for each population marker settled (free for each factory)
    • Place all the pieces on the board, and gain one production for each piece placed (each color factory placed, and each color population marker covered up). Production is tracked by a separate disk for each resource on the new world produciton track.
    • The stronger biomes have markers on population spots that indicate multiple production level gains
  • Infrastructure (right of 3, left of 1)
    • Each building icon lets you take 1 infrastructure action
    • An infrastructure action is taking a card or paying the resources to play an infrastructure card to the new world
    • Like with other markets, you have an optional refresh
    • Each infrastructure card is a tile placement object you'll be trying to complete to get the production bonuses shown on the card
      • Some of these are as simple as have a number of settled spaces in specified terrains
      • Some are as complicated as have settled specific terrains in a straight line
    • An infrastructure card can be played regardless if you currently meet the condidtion or not. Once it's in play you gain the rewards immediately whenever you fufill the conditions on a later turn (or if you meet it now). You'll gain the new world production shown on the card.
    • You can only gain the bonus of a card once, and you cant' build multiple cards with the same illustration (an exclaimation mark up top indicates duplicates in the deck)

docs.title: Passing
docs.text:
• If you can't afford to take another action, or just don't want to, you must pass.
  • Move your turn order marker down and out of the line up. Your turn will be skipped the rest of the action phase.
  • Once all players have passed, we'll move on to the transport phase

docs.title: Transport
docs.text:
How you use your spaceships to transport resources and buildings to the new world. Empty ships will return from the new world to the old.

  • You may transport with any number of old world ships. For each ship used, pay the energy cost to fuel it shown on the left
  • Evacuation bay (the half cirlces)
    • For each half circle on the card (in between cost and capacity) you may evacuate a matching colored old world site
    • Choose an old world site (circle token) showing the same color as the half circle indicates
    • If it's population, you would flip over the token on the old world and put the indicated number of population markers onto the ship
    • if its a factory, flip over the token on the old world and put a matching factory from your supply onto the ship
    • A factory put in your ship using the evacuation bay icon does not count against the factory capacity of the ship

  • Ship capacity

    • You can also transport factories, stadiums, and resource cubes matching the capcity shown in the top right
    • You can load a factory as part of the building capacity and not use an evacuation bay for it (this is the only way to remove factories that don't have an evac bay color on the site)

  • You may also transport empty ships from the new world back to the old world

    • This is a cost of 1 energy per ship
    • They can never hold any goods, will always be empty

  • Once all decisions have been made, move ships to their destinations

    • All energy paid for fuel costs are returned to the supply
    • All stuff brought to the new world is moved out of the ships onto that side of the board

docs.title: Turn order
docs.text:
• Add up happiness (smiley face icons) from new world, old world, and tech track
  • Re-arrange players on turn order track in order from happiest to least happy
  • If tied, swap relative order

docs.title: Progress
docs.text:
• Add up total power of all actions taken that round (number in action slot and on cards played for effect)
  • In new turn order, each player will move their progress markers a combined number of spaces equal to that power
  • Only green spaces count. The black spaces are bonuses whenever you pass them, but don't count as a space on the track
  • You can move your markers at different paces, but can never have more than one transition space inbetwen your two markers
  • You will initially move along the "passing lane" which is the black stripe. If your marker ends its movement on a segment where there are circle icons present in the green bands of the track, you can move your marker to one of those circles to gain the rewards.
  • Only a single player may occupy each of these bonus spaces.
  • All future progress movement phases will begin by moving all tokens back to the passing lane. Meaning whoever goes first will have full choice of any bonuses, even in previously full spots
  • At two players, only the inner lane is available for placement
  • Passing the first black "bonus" space means you flip over one of those tokens on the old world, losing that produciton
  • When your second marker passes the halfway point, you'll reclaim one of those tokens onto the new world side of your playerboard and increase each of your productions by one. The token does not need to be settled to gain this effect.
  • You'll gain the second token whenever both your progress markers have crossed the forest build space.

docs.title: Bonus check
docs.text: Friendly bonus, get the lower one no matter what, get the higher one if you meet it

docs.title: General
docs.text:
We play strangers venturing into the legendary city-state of Naqala to maneuver the local tribes (spoilers, there's five of them), invoke Djinns and gain influence. At the end of the game the player with the most points is crowned Sultan and wins

All money is private in this game.

docs.title: Bidding for Turn order
docs.text:
We'll start each round by bidding for the order that we'll take turns in.

The player whose marker is in the 1 spot on the bid order track will move their marker to a space on the turn order track, paying the price shown.

If you bid zero, you're placed in the bold zero spot. Anyone that bids zero after you will cause your piece to bump down into a lower zero spot.

docs.title: Player actions
docs.text:
In this turn order we'll do the following actions on our turn

  • Move turn order marker off of the turn track and put it on the first empty spot on the bid order track

docs.title: Move meeples
docs.text:
Select a tile that has at least one meeple. Take all the meeples from that tile and drop them onto adjacent tiles one at a time, following these rules:

  • The last meeple you place must be on a tile with at least 1 meeple of the same color
  • You can't place meeples onto diagonal spaces
  • You can't immediately back track, but can circle back around

You can pass through empty tiles placing one meeple there as you move across, but you may not end your meeple placement on an empty tile

Once finished placing meeples, take the last meeple you placed on the final tile back into your hand, along with all matching meeples of that color from that space.

If removing all the meeples of this color leaves the tile empty, you place a camel onto the space to show that you control it. This will get you points at the end of the game

docs.title: Tribes actions
docs.text:
All the meeples are from different tribes that do different actions. Depending on the color meeples you grabbed from the last tile, you'll do a different action

  • Yellow
    • Place in front of you, worth VP at end of game
  • White
    • Place in front of you, worth VP at end of game
    • Can also be spent to gain Djinn and use their powers
  • Green
    • Place back in bag
    • Grab a number of resource cards from the far end of the line equal to the number of meeples
    • Don't replace cards
  • Blue
    • Place back in bag
    • Look for blue point tiles surrounding yours up, down, left, right and diagonal (counting your tile should be 9)
    • Take the number of blue point tiles times the number of meeples you picked up and gain that much gold
    • May discard Fakir cards from your hand to add +1 to the multiplyer
  • Red
    • Place back in bag
    • Kill one meeple within range, removing it from its tile and putting it in the bag
    • Range is up to a number of tiles away equal to the number of meeples (can't count diagonal)
    • OR kill a yellow or white meeple in front of another player, putting it back in bag
    • May discard Fakir cards to increase range by +1 each
    • Killing the last meeple on a tile claims it for you, so you put a camel on it

docs.title: Tile action
docs.text:
Once you've distributed meeples and taken the tribe action, you'll do the tile action

  • Perform the action of the tile you placed your last meeple on. TIles with a red arrow are a MUST, others are MAY
  • Oasis: Place a plam tree on the tile, it is now worth more end game points. No limit to number of trees on a single tile (components are limited)
  • Palace: Place a palace tile, it is now worth more end game points. No limit to number of palaces except components
  • Cards: Pay the money indicated, then take 1 card from the first 3 cards of the line up, or 2 cards from the first 6 cards of the line up. Don't replace cards.
  • Djinn: Pay either 2 white meeples or 1 meeple and a Fakir to take a Djinn card of your choice from the line up
    • Don't replace yet
    • Djinn give you end game points
    • Some have special powers for free or a cost. Ones with a cost can only be used once per turn
    • You can use a power the turn you get a Djinn (as long as you can pay it)

docs.title: Sell goods (optional)
docs.text:
As the last action on your turn you may sell goods (but not Fakir)

The number of unique cards in the set determines how much money you get.

You may sell as many sets as you want.

docs.title: Clean up
docs.text:
Once all players have taken a turn, refill all Djinn and goods. Before refilling goods, slide them all up to the front of the row and then draw up to 9

Now we bid for turn order for the next round

docs.title: End of the game
docs.text:
Two ways for the game to end:

  • A player drops their last camel
  • There are no legal Meeple movements possible

docs.title: Scoring
docs.text:
• 1 VP per gold meeple
  • 1 VP per yellow meeple
    • +10 points for each opponent with less than you
  • 2 VP per white meeple
  • Sum of all VP on your Djinn
  • 3 VP for each palm tree on tiles with your camels
  • 5 VP for each palace on tiles with your cames
  • Sum of all VP on tiles with your camels
  • Sum of VP for goods left in hand

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
We are different races trying to peacefully colonize the galaxy. Each race has different environmental needs, which has lead all the races to master terraforming. We will colonize new planets, build structures, and unite planets to form federations. The game is played over 6 rounds, and at the end, the player with the most victory points will win.

Some sources of victory points are the round scoring tiles, which will award points during each round for taking different actions, the 2 end game times, which will award points based on how well players fulfilled certain criteria, federation tokens, tech tree progress, and some tech tiles.

Each round consists of 4 phases. Income, Gaia, actions, and clean up. We will all take income and Gaia phase, then take turns doing one action each in turn order. After everyone has passed, we will perform cleanup and move to the next round

During income, every hand symbol will reward you with resources. Ore (white) and knowledge (blue)can go up to 15, credits can go up to 30. You can also receive power.and charge power as income

docs.title: The power cycle
docs.text:
Purple area of board split into different numbered areas. You can only spend power in area 3 of your board. To get it there, you’ll have to charge it. (Point out charge power icon) When you charge power, it has to be moved from area 1 to 2, and then only when area 2 is empty, to area 3. You can't charge power if all of it is already in area 3. Spending power moves it from area 3 back to area 1.

There are also effects that allow you to gain or discard power. When gaining power, grab it from the supply and put it in area 1. When discarding power, you can remove from anywhere except Gaia area. More on the Gaia area later

docs.title: Income
docs.text: During the income phase of your turn, you will gain all bonuses that show the income symbol, which looks like an extra hand (point out on mine track)

docs.title: Build a mine
docs.text:
Mines are the only structure that can be directly built on a planet to colonize them. Cost is shown by mines, 2 credits and one ore. Can only build if the follow criteria are met:

  • Planet is empty (no structures)
  • It is accessible from one of your planets
  • It is habitable by your faction. If not, you must pay to terraform.

docs.title: Accessibility
docs.text:
Determined by your progress on the navigation tech track (gray). At levels 0 and 1, your basic range is one, so you can only build on adjacent planets. Once you increase it, it is always up to that value, you can use less

  • When counting distance, you just count the shortest path between one of your planets and where you int and to build. This path can be a straight line through other planets (even colonized ones)
  • Additionally, you can always spend one QIC (little green cube) to increase your distance by 2

docs.title: Habitability
docs.text:
Your home planet type can be naturally built on (the one of your player color). Otherwise, you will need a number of terraforming steps as indicated by the chart on your player board. The cost of each terraforming step is determined by your progress on terraforming tech track. Starts at 3 ore per step.

  • Purple planets (trans dimm) can't be built on. They can be converted to a green planet by using a gaiformer.
  • Green planets can be built on by paying a QIC

docs.title: Upgrade building
docs.text:
You can only ever build mines onto empty planets. All other structures have to be upgraded into. The connections or upgrade paths are shown on your player board.

When an upgraded structure is placed, the original structure is placed back on your player board in the rightmost position. Just like mines, building are pulled from the leftmost space.

  • Mine > trading station (3/6 + 2)
    • Discounted credits if there is an opponents neighboring structure (range of 2 away)
  • Trading station > planetary institute (6 + 4)
    • Planetary institute immediately allows access to an asymmetric faction power.
  • Trading station > research lab (5 + 3)
    • Gain tech tile
  • Research lab > academy (6 + 6)
    • You choose which to build, also gain tech tile

docs.title: Gaining tech tiles
docs.text:
Tech tiles can gain you a number of benefits, and when you grab one, you will advance in a tech tree as well. Most of the time, you’ll be grabbing a basic tech tile.

If you grab a tech tile from directly below a tree, advance in that tree. If you grab on of the 3 not associated with a tree, go up on any tree.

You can grab any basic tech you want, but can't have multiples.

Instead of taking a basic, you can take an advanced if you meet some conditions. When you grab an advanced tile, you go up one on any track.

  • Must be at level 4 or 5 in that research track
  • Must own a federation token green side up. You will then flip to gray
  • Must have an uncovered standard tile. You will then cover the standard tile, blocking it's effect

docs.title: Research
docs.text:
You can pay 4 knowledge to go up on a research track of your choice. Going up to level 5 also requires you to flip a federation token to its grey side. Only one person can be in level 5 at one time. Some new levels will give you a one time bonus (white outline), some will give you passive benefits.

  • Brown is terraforming efficacy
  • Grey is navigation (range)
  • Green is AI (gets you QIC)
  • Purple is Gaia project
  • Yellow is economy (income)
  • Blue is science (knowledge income)

docs.title: Federation
docs.text:
To get federation tiles, you have to make a federation (or a group) of your planets. You can make as many as you want in a game, but each planet can only be a part of one federation.

You form federations with planets you have built structures on with a total power of 7 or more. Planets that are directly adjacent are connected, for other planets you will have to discard one power each to build satellites (represented by cubes) to connect them.

A space can only hold one satellite of any player color, but multiple players cubes can be in the same space. You cannot choose to make a federation with unnecessary planets added, you must use as few satellites as possible.

docs.title: Power and QIC actions
docs.text:
The bottom of the research board shows purple and green spaces. To take the action in a purple space, spend the required amount of power (from area 3). To take a green action, spend the depicted amount of qics.

Each power and QIC action can only be taken once per round. These will be blocked off with an action token after being taken.

docs.title: Special actions
docs.text: Orange spaces indicate a special action. Unlike the other colored octagon spaces, these have no cost and are available only to the player with the component they appear on. These can also only be used once per round and will be blocked off with an action tile after use.

docs.title: Free actions and power charge
docs.text:
In addition to your main action, you can take any number of free actions on your turn.

Discard a power token to move power from area 2 to area 3 ( this can be done even if you have power tokens in area 1)

Conversion chart on your board are all free actions

Whenever someone builds a power in the neighboring area of one of your structures (2 away) you may spend vo to charge power. You pay vp based on how much power you would charge (the power value of the building). 0/1/2/3 vp for 1/2/3/4 power charged. If you have multiple buildings, you only charge the highest power one.

You must pay for all of it unless you either only able to charge fewer power than the max, or can't pay for all of it. Otherwise you are not allowed to shortchange just to spend less.

docs.title: Passing and round boosters
docs.text:
When you pass, you're out for the round. When you pass, take one of the 3 available round booster tiles, then return the one you held. You must swap, you can't use the same booster across multiple rounds by holding onto it.

Place the new booster facedown in front of you to show that you've already passed.

Round boosters that show an icon for activating when you pass are triggered the round you RETURN the booster.

Whoever passes first also becomes the new first player.

docs.title: Gaia Action
docs.text: Read from book

docs.title: End game
docs.text:
Talk about final scoring tiles and tracking 1/2/3

Research track scoring

Resource scoring

Reiterate round scoring tiles

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
We're ranchers in the west who want to make their fortunes selling cattle to the east. This game has deck building and hand management, where everything is in service of working your way along the trail to go sell cattle at market at the top of the board and ship them off to different cities. We'll also utilize different types of workers to construct buildings, buy new cows, and move our train around more.

There is no victory point track, all points are awarded at the end of the game.

docs.title: Turn structure:
docs.text: On your turn you'll do 3 things. Move, take actions in the space you land in, and then draw up to your handsize. There are spaces on your player hard to remind you of how these work

docs.title: Movement:
docs.text:
• Your base movement is listed on your board (4 in a 4p game), but you can increase that via upgrades.
  • You must always move forward at least 1 space, and you can never go backwards.
  • It takes one movement point per location you move across. Locations are tiles, any empty building site does not require movement to cross.
  • You can share spaces, and Kansas City (at the end of the trail) stops all movement.
  • The space where you end up is the one you activate during the next part of your turn

Green and black hands:

  • These are shown on tiles you move over and indicate fees that you have to pay.
  • Green is one dollar, black is two.
  • Hazard (point to hazard tiles) or outlaw tiles (point to outlaws) get paid to the bank, player tile fees get paid to that player.
  • Not being able to pay does not stop your movement. Pay what you can, if anything, and keep moving. Never have to pay retroactivly. So if you land on a space you can't pay for but then earn money, you don't have to then pay the fee you missed.

docs.title: Activate tile actions
docs.text:
• If it's a neutral building or owned by you, activate either all location actions (actions on tile), or one auxiliary action.
  • The symbol for a single auxiliary action is a single gear, a double auxiliary action symbol is a double gear.
  • auxiliary actions are listed on the side of your playerboard, but we'll come back to those.
  • Local actions can be done in any order, you don't have to do them all. Some have a cost, some have a slash for either/or, most have 1-3 options
  • Many action spaces will cost a card or cards from your hand to do something on the tile. The tile will show a color of cattle that you must discard from your hand to gain the benefit show. No cattle means no action.
  • If you land on a hazard, outlaw, or tile owned by another player, you can only take a single auxiliary action.
  • So you want to try and stop on neutral buildings or ones you own to get the most out of your turn

docs.title: Draw up to hand size
docs.text:
• At the end of your turn you will draw up to your max hand size
  • Starts at 4, but can be upgraded

docs.title: Kansas City
docs.text:
There is one space you can end your turn on that we haven't talked about, Kansas City. This is where you send your cattle to market, make money, and return to the start of the trail. The reminder steps for this are on the board near the Kansas city stop

When you reach Kansas City, you must carry out the 5 steps in order

  • Phase 1-3: Foresights: pick one of the two tiles and place it in the appropriate section.
    • Hazards and outlaws go in the next lowest numbered space in the appropriate area.
    • Workers get placed in their area following a few rules. Must be placed in the row with the job market token left to right. When the job market token would be covered move it down.
    • This is also the game timer, when it reaches the end, the end of the game is triggered. Additionally when it reaches the halfway point, the cattle market is refilled.
  • Phase 4: income:
    • Reveal your hand
    • Calculate your total breeding value by adding up the values from every unique cow in your hand.
    • Duplicates will not be counted.
    • You can increase your breeding value by using certificates:
    • (point to the certificates on your player board)
    • There are temporary certificates that you have a tracker for on your board, you can use them by moving the cube down or permanent ones that you can unlock
    • Once your breeding value is set, grab that much money and then discard your hand
  • Phase 5: Deliver cattle to a city
    • You must now pick a city to deliver to based on your total breeding value (equal or lower)
    • Can't deliver to a city with your disk on it, with the exception of Kansas City and New York.
    • Once you pick a city, select a disk from your board to place. This is how you can upgrade things like your board movement, hand size, certificate limit, and auxiliary actions.
    • Keep in mind the color space you take the disk from must match the color City you deliver to (black or white).
    • You will then gain any bonuses shown on the city, and may gain or lose points at the end of the game
    • Bonuses in green arrows aren't triggered unless you have a disk in both locations
    • Lastly, check to see if you need to pay transport costs. If your train is directly at the city or further up the track, it's free. If your train is further back, pay 1 for each crossing symbol between you and the destination city
  • Move your herder to the start of the trail horse space
  • Refill the forsight spaces from the appropriate bags

docs.title: Actions
docs.text: We'll talk about some of the actions that are on the starting tiles, and that will touch upon some of the other major concepts of the game

docs.title: Hiring workers
docs.text:
Whenever you do this action you will hire a worker from the job market and move them to your player board.

  • You can't hire a worker from the row with the job marker, only rows above
  • The cost to hire has a base price of the row you're taking the worker from
  • The action to get your worker can have a price modifier, red being an additional cost, green being a discount
  • The worker is placed in the matching row on your playerboard
  • Placing a worker may grant you a bonus or additional action
  • You may not hire a worker if the row is full already
  • Workers on the final 2 spaces of each track are worth end game points

Worth noting that you start with one of each worker (the printed ones on the player board)

docs.title: Buying cattle
docs.text:
You starting cows aren't great, there is a market where you can buy better cows that are all also worth end game points

  • The number of cowboys that you have dictates what options you can take with this action
  • All of the available actions are shown at the bottom of the board in the cattle market
  • Cowboys are "spent" whenever you perform cattle market actions (but not removed from your board). You can only use each cowboy once
  • So with 2 cowboys, you could use a single 2 cowboy action, or you could perform 2 single cowboy actions
  • You can always buy fewer cows than you are able
  • Any cow you buy goes to your discard pile
  • The cattle market will not refill until the halfway point of the game
  • You can use a single cowboy to add 2 cards from the cattle market deck and add them to the lineup

docs.title: Simmental variant
docs.text:
Adds a new color cow, orange!

  • These have a market action as well, and will upgrade whenever they're in your hand when you go to Kansas City
  • Start worth little breeding value and points as a level one cow
  • Can upgrade to level 2 and then level 3, worth more each time

docs.title: Moving your train
docs.text:
You have to move your train down the line to deliver to bigger cities without having to pay out the ass

  • The number of engineers you have dictates how far you can move your train
  • Some action spaces show a symbol for 1 train movement for each engineer you have
  • Some action spaces just show fixed train movement
  • Train movement is always UP TO the value
  • Each space on the train track can only hold one train. You skip over other trains ahead of you when counting movement
  • There are a number of turnout spaces where you can veer off the main track to a train station
  • When choosing to move a fewer number of train spaces, you lose you extra movement. So you can't stop a train station, do a thing, and then continue moving along

Upgrading a train station

  • The reason you may want to veer off the main track is to upgrade a train station
  • You can only upgrade a train station if you can pay the upgrade cost shown to the right of the space and don't already have a disk there
  • This is the only other way to get disks off of your player board to upgrade things
  • There is also a station master tile. You can get this tile by appointing one of your workers as the station master.
  • You remove the worker from your board and swap them with the station master tile. This means only one player can get each tile
  • You only have the option to do this the turn you visit a train station. You can't do it on a later turn.
  • There are train stations that only allow you to upgarde and drop a disk, but don't have a tile
  • The last space on the train track is special. If your movement ends there you can upgrade or not, but then you gain 3 dollars and move your train to any space on the train track that you want. If you choose to move it to a turnout space, you can immediately upgrade that space like normal
  • Some train stations are worth points at the end of the game if you have your disk there

docs.title: Place a private building
docs.text:
You have a number of private buildings that you can build onto the board

  • The number of builders you have dictates what you can build
  • We're all playing with buildings on the same side, you can't decide to flip it over and build that instead
  • You can build onto an empty board space, or replace one of your own buildings
  • To build a building, you must have enough builders for the building and must be able to pay for it
    • On the building tile the top left shows the number of builders required
    • The action you take to build will show a cost of coins per builder used

Alternatively, you can overbuild

  • This replaces a building you already have on the trail with a higher valued building
  • To do this, you only need the difference in builders
  • You also only need to pay the difference
  • So with a 2 builder tile already on the board with a 4 builder tile, you would have to have 2 workers and pay 4 coins.
  • This method allows you to build buildings that require more workers than you actually have
  • Buildings are worth points at the end of the game

docs.title: Objective cards
docs.text:
Everyone's starting objective begins in their starting objective area. The rest of the objective cards work a little different.

There are actions that let you gain an objective card from the display, or draw the top card of the stack.

  • The objective card goes into your discard pile
  • On a later turn when it is in your hand, you can play it
  • Can play before moving or before or after performing tile actions
  • Can't play in the middle of an action or when you go to draw up to hand size
  • When you play an objective card it goes into your personal objective area
  • You also get to perform the action on the top left of the card

Each objective card shows a combination of tasks to fufill at the end of the game

  • Each fufilled task can count only towards one objective card
  • So if you have 2 objective cards in your area that each want you to have built a single building, you need at least 2 total buildings at the end of the game

Fufilling all tasks on a card gets you the positive points at the end of the game. Not fufilling them all gets you negative points.

You can keep objective cards in your deck without playing them, discarding them through various means. At the end of the game you get the option to pull them out of your deck to score them or not where you are forced to score everything in your objective area .

docs.title: Auxiliary actions
docs.text:
The side of your player board shows all of your possible auxiliary actions. Some actions start unlocked (ones that show a 1x) and some start locked (ones with a disk)

  • If at least one disk is removed, the action is unlocked
  • Whenever you see the single gear icon, that means perform any single unlocked auxiliary action
  • Whenever you see the double gear, that means choose an auxiliary action and perform it. If only one disk is unlocked you can only do it once, but if both disks are unlocked you get to do it twice
  • Some auxilliary actions have a cost. Paying money is simple, but some make you move your train backward
    • You must move the full movement backwards
    • You skip counting spaces with other trains
    • You may move backwards into a turnout space and upgrade it as usual
    • Can't do this if you're on the starting train space

Explain the auxiliary actions on the board

docs.title: Expansion action: branchlet
docs.text:
There is a new board next to yours with a bunch of little buildings called branchlets.

  • You have a new auxiliary action
  • Pay 1 dollar and discard a cattle card with a breeding value of two and place a branchlet.
  • Double action means you pay 2 and discard 2 cards to place 2 branchlets.
  • Take the branclet from the leftmost space of the top available row on your board
  • Place in any free branchlet space available to you
  • A space is considered available if at least one rail track connects it directly to one of the following
    • Kansas city
    • A city crest on the city strip that has one of your disks
    • Another town square that has one of your branchlets
  • You can only have one branchlet in any town square
  • Pay the cost shown to place, or gain the bonus shown when placing branchlet
  • Check the type of town you built in:
    • Small towns grant no additional action
    • Station towns allow you to upgrade the train station, and potentially take the station master tile
    • Medium towns allow you to take the action shown on their square tile
    • Big towns have city crests attacted. Having a branchlet on a big town allows you to deliver cattle to it later.

docs.title: Misc actions
docs.text:
• Removing an outlaw: grab an outlaw token to get the reward shown on the space. Can't do this if there are no outlaws
  • Grab an exchange token: lets you get a token you can spend to draw up to two cards and then discard that may cards
  • Removing a hazard: Take a hazad off the board from any section. Worth points at the end of the game

docs.title: End of the game
docs.text:
The game end is triggered when a worker is placed into the last space of the job market when someone goes to Kansas city.

  • The player who triggered this grabs the job market token and it is worth 2 VP.
  • That player gets no more turns
  • Each other player gets 1 final turn
  • During these final turns, workers are not placed in the job market, but the first foresight tiles are still placed on the board like normal

docs.title: Scoring
docs.text:
• 1 point for every 5 dollars
  • Sum of points from personal buildings
  • Sum of points from disks on delivered cities
  • Sum of points from upgraded train stations
  • Sum of points from personal deck
  • Sum of points from objective cards in your scoring area (pulling any cards out of deck that you want to add)
  • Points from station master tiles
  • Points from workers on player board
  • Points from movement space on player board
  • Points if you have the job token

docs.title: Inferno
docs.text:
Note

We all play different familys in Florence that are accusing sinners and sending them to Hell. Once they’re in hell, we will make sure the sinners get to their correct circle of hell. You win by getting the most infanmy points. The game was inspired by Dante Alighieri’s Divine Comedy

docs.title: Gameplay
docs.text:
The game is played over a number of rounds that are made up of 2 phases. The Hell phase, where we move souls though the circles of hell, and the Florence phase, where we move our family members around the city above. The game will end after Dante reaches the final step on his descent into hell.

The main board is split in to 2 parts, Florence above, and Hell below. Below the layers of hell are the sin tracks.

docs.title: Turn overview
docs.text: Each turn is divided into 2 mandatory parks.

docs.title: Hell Phase
docs.text:
You will be leading souls to the same-colored circle of hell for punishment. This earns you infamy points, with higher values the deeper you go.

To move a soul in Hell, you can:

  • Move a soul already in hell that has yet to reach the same colored circle. These will be standing up, while souls in their correct cirlce will be laying down
  • Take a soul from the graveyard and place it on any sqace in the first circle. To do so, the soul must cross the river and you must pay Charon one Drachma.

If you cannot move any soul in Hell and you have no Drachmas to move one from the graveyard, you must take out a loan to get the money to move a soul across the river.

docs.title: Loans
docs.text:
• Whenever you need Drachmas, you can take out a loan for infamy points
  • Move your boat down one space on the loan track and gain 2 Drachmas
  • If your boat passes the instant effect symbol, you immediately lose 5 infamy points
  • If you are on the alst space already, don't move your boat but still gain Drachmas and lose the points
  • No limit to # of loans, but you will lose points at the end of the game for your position on this track

docs.title: Soul movement
docs.text:
• Souls can only move along the lines leading form their current space
  • If moving to an empty sheild in the next layer down, a souls movement ends
  • If a soul would move into a space already occupied, it moves through that space further down, again along the lines
  • It is possible to chain several movements this way
  • Once a soul reaches its same colored layer, lay it down and gain the points associated with that layer.
  • Laid down souls can still be moved through, but they themselves cannot be moved

docs.title: Wal of dis
docs.text:
• To move from the 5th circle to the 6th cirlce, you must move through the wall.
  • To do this, you must have previously placed a safe passage (one of your discs) on the gate of the wall above the destination shield
  • Must be your disk, cannot use other players safe passages

docs.title: Sudden Death
docs.text: If during the hell phase, you cannot move a soul, the game ends immediately. This is a different endgame trigger that shouldn't come up.

docs.title: Florence Phase
docs.text:
The Florence section of the board is broken up into different quarters, with two locations in each. The shields in these quarters are the same symbols on all of the hell movements. Whatever symbol the soul you moved in the Hell pahse of your turn ended on is the quarter of the board that you will take your actions in for the Florence phase of your turn. You can bribe Charon 2 Drachmas to take your turn in a different quarter. The different quarters have 2 locations

  • Square locations with no access requirement.

  • Rounded locations with access requirements shown in the top left setion of their tiles. To accuse sinners here there must be a guest in your tower who matches the color of that location

  • Send a family memeber to florence

Take a family memeber from your home (not the tower), place it on a location, and perform that location's action. Your family memebers consist of 4 nobles (large meeples) and 4 urchins (small meeples). The nobles start the game on your board, the urchins do not.

  • The location must be in the quadrant that matches the symbol where the soul moved in the hell phase ended

docs.title: Free access locations:
docs.text:
• Haystack (purple)
    • Place an urchin from your reserve next to the family member you just placed here
    • This is how you get more workers
  • Banquet (green)
    • Take the next tower floor from your reserve and place it in your tower
    • Expanding tower allows more guests, store more barrels, and shelter more family members
  • Bank (yellow)
    • Receive 2 Florins plus one for each guest (not family member) housed in your tower
    • Florins allow you to access some locations or can be converted into Drachmas
  • Courtyard (blue)
    • Perform up to two optional actions:
      • Transfer 1 or 2 of your family members from your tower to your home
      • Reorganize your towers: Arrange the guests, barrels and family memebers however you like without adding or removing anything

docs.title: Special locations:
docs.text:
• Bonfire (yellow):
    • Perform up to 3 optional actions:
      • Exhange up to 3 florins for drachmas
      • Choose between moving a phlegethon cube one space to the right OR placing a safe passage disc on the wall
      • Choose between paying 1 drachma to move a guardian and play its effects OR Pay 2 drachmas and play the river styx to advance on 1 or 2 sin tracks (its own area of the board that lets you remove a number of same colored souls in the graveyard to go up on sin tracks)
  • Wall (red):
    • Perform up to 3 optional actions:
      • Exhange up to 2 florins into drachmas
      • Pay 2 drachmas and play a fraud card
      • Move a Barrel from your tower to its space on the supply pannel for an instant reward of infamy points based on different criteria
  • Market (gray):
    • Place a barrel from the market on an empty space in your tower
    • You can never have more than one barrel of the same color
  • Palace (blue):
    • Take a Guest from this location and place them on an empty space in your tower, earning florings equal to the height of the floor (0-4).
    • You can only have one guest of each color
    • Guests allow you to accuse sinners that match their color

docs.title: Fraud cards:
docs.text:
• Panel on left is all instant. Requirement to play the card up top (barrel located anywhere), choice of reward on botrtom
  • Panel on the right is all endgame. Requirement to score it on top, endgame effects on bottom
  • Barrel requirements are just a check, they are not consumed
  • When you get the action to play a fraud card, you can play it from your hand or from the lineup, or from the top of the deck. Playing from anywhere but hand requires paying 1 drachma.

  • If you play from hand, draw a new card

  • Accuse a sinner

Each location contains sinners of a specific type. There are no game pieces representing sinners. When one is accused and executed, they turn into a soul, represented by a meeple in the graveyard. Each sin is represented by the same color across the game. - To accuse you must already have a family member at the location where you want to accuse a sinner and meet the requirements of that location - You can only accuse in the quandrant where you finished the hell phase or spend 2 drachma to choose a different quadrant

Steps for accusing

Choose a family memeber at a location and follow these steps in order:

  1. Move the family memeber who accused to an empty space in your tower. If the accusation was in a special location, send them to the floor where the guest matching that color is housed.
  2. If you have any other family members in Florence, teturn them to your home.
  3. Put a soul from the supply of the correct color into the graveyard
  4. Move your skull up one level on the sin track cooresponding to the color of the moved soul (point out the sin track). Obtain the bonus indicated on the bonus tile to the left of that sin track

  5. Move the Dante meeple down a step, activating the reward on his step

    • Place a pagan soul (cream) into the graveyard. You may optionally play a guardian
    • Play a fraud card
    • Award deploma at Dante's current level. THe player with the higest positioned skull on the sin track that matches Dante's current level places a diploma in the diploma area of the same color. In case of a tie, all players do this.
    • (last space) Earn 2 points, award the ice blue diploma, proceed to end game

  6. Family council

When you have no more family memebers to move to Florence, and you can't accuse a sinner, you must take this action. - Lose 2 infamy points and choose an option: - Move a family member to your home from your tower or a location - Return a guest from your tower to the Palace - Return a barrel from your tower to the Market

docs.title: ENDGAME
docs.text:
Whenever someone accuses and forces Dante to move to the final step and award the final diploma, that is their last turn. All other players get one final turn.

It is realistic to expect 1/3 of your infamy points from placing souls in hell, and earn 1/3 of your points from diplomas.

docs.title: POINTS
docs.text:
1. Scoring fraud cards
    • If you have the barrels end game sides of cards require, activate them
  2. Adjust river phlegethon score
    • cubes will move one space to the left for each empty shield at that level.
  3. Gain additional diplomas. For every 2 half diploma tokens you have you get to place a dimploma where you don't already have one.
  4. Score diplomas. Mutiply score on plegethon cube by the level where your skull is. This will provide between 1 to 20 points.
  5. Repay your loans. Each person loses points indicated on the space where their boat is.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
This is a bag building game where we're rival train companies building a shared network of routes and stations to deliver goods to

On a turn you'll pull chips from your bag, and those chips will determine what you can do on your turn. You'll use these chips to build railsway, stations, and deliver goods. You can also buy new chips to make your bag better

docs.title: Round structure
docs.text:
At the start of the round we'll reveal a production card which will pop some goods out on to the map.

We will then award subsidies if necessary. The point track is broken down into segments. If you have an entire segment between you and the person in first place, you'll get a lightning train. You can get a max of two lightning trains this way.

Getting a lightning train just means that you'll pull one of the train chips from your supply, but more on that later.

Each player will then take one turn. We will keep playing rounds like this until the end of the game is triggered.

A turn is broken down into two major parts:

  • The operations phase, where you will take actions
  • The turnover phase, where you will clean up for the next turn

docs.title: The playerboard and chips
docs.text: Before jumping in to how to do things, lets talk about the playerboard and how tiles work since you'll be using them for everything.

docs.title: Boarding area
docs.text: The leftmost portion of your board is the boarding area, where you will put tiles that you draw from your bag. For your turn, you will draw tiles out of your bag until the 5 blank empty spaces are filled up. If when pulling chips out of your bag you pull chips that have the plus symbol on them, they will slot into the spaces with the plus icon. Any time you get a lightning train (from a chip or other source), you will grab a basic train chip from your supply and put it in the boarding area on the lightning symbol so it won't take up any slots for normal chips. So plus symbols and lightning train symbols will allow you to go over the normal 5 chip limit

docs.title: Railyard
docs.text:
All the empty spaces on your playerboard are your railyard actions.

  • These spaces can only be filled with train chips
  • You move train chips from your boarding area to empty action spaces
  • Some actions show a little red banner with a station, like the Secure Contract action.
    • This means you can not assign train chips (or take the action) unless you have at least that number of stations built
  • To take an action:
    • All spaces for that action must be filled with train chips
    • You must remove the train chips from those spaces and set them in your warehouse area (off to the side of your board on the right)
    • You are not required to take the action now. Train chips can stay on railyard actions between turns, so you can load up an action now to take on a later turn
  • You MUST assign any remaining trains in your boarding area to empty railyard spaces if able before ending your turn

docs.title: Build a station
docs.text:
You start the game with six cubes at the top of your board, these are your station markers.

To build a station, you must meet 2 criteria:

  • Have a contract for the region where you will be building the station (more on this in a moment)
  • Each city shows a cost to build a station there, you must spend that many train chips from the railyard action

If you meet those criteria, then:

  • Choose a face up station from one of the slots on the board and move it to the location where you're building
  • Take the leftmost station marker from your board and put it on the station
  • Refill the station slot w/ one of the same size from the supply

docs.title: Secure Contracts
docs.text: One of the criteria to build things like a station is having a contract. Everone starts with a couple hybrid contracts that are one time use. The color and letter on the contract cooresponds to the zone of the board that it allows you to build in. Starting contracts are lost after using, but the secure contract action on your board allows you to act as if you had any contract for the rest of your turn.

docs.title: Building a rail line
docs.text:
This is not an action on your playerboard, but it makes sense to talk about it here.

A rail line is a series of train chips played on an empty path between cities on the game board. Rail lines are used to make deliveries and score points

You may only build 1 rail line per turn. To build a rail line, you must meet the following requirements:

  • You must have a contract for the region where you want to build. If the line crosses a region boundary, a contract for either region will suffice.
  • You must be able to fill every slot of the path using train chips
    • Generally, these will all come from your boarding area or line storage
    • If you have a station in the city on either end of the rail line, you may use a rail chip from your supply (max of 1 even if you own cities on both sides)
  • The rail line must have a connection to one of the following:
    • One of the starting cities on the eastern seaboard with the sun symbol
    • A city where you have a station
    • One of your existing rail lines
  • If there is a mountain path (paths marked with brown slots) you must be able to tunnel by having a tunnel icon from one of these sources:
    • A chip in your boarding area
    • An action card you've played
    • Or by taking the use explosives railyard action

If you meet all the requirements, put out the train chips and then score points equal to the number of stars on one end of the link.

Now that we've coverd a lot of the concepts, lets take a look at the rest of the railyard actions

docs.title: Line Storage
docs.text: Not an action you take on it's own. You store a train chip here to use on a later turn whenever you build a rail line as part of that line

docs.title: Use Explosives
docs.text:
When you use this action, you get one of two effects:

  • Get a tunnel symbol for this turn to build on mountain paths
  • Build an additional rail line this turn (normal limit is one)

If you had enough train chips, you could fill this space up and remove trains multiple times in a turn to build more than 2 rail lines

docs.title: Industrialize
docs.text: Requires 2 stations. When you take this action, flip that board over and you now have two more + spaces in your boarding area

docs.title: Going public
docs.text:
Requires 2 stations, gets you 2 new passive bonuses

  • Whenever you generate 7 money or more on your turn, you score two points
  • For every delivery icon you get 1 lightning train

docs.title: Deliveries
docs.text:
Another action not on your player board. A delivery uses a series of rail lines to move goods to a city that wants them from the closest connected producer city.

You may make any number of deliveries on your turn, but each delivery requires either:

  • A delivery icon on a chip in your boarding area or a card you've played
  • Spending one of the delivery tokens that you start the game with

To make a delivery, follow these steps: - Choose a station with at least one goods request - Identify the closest connected producer city with a face up goods token corresponding to the request - Connected means there must be an unbroken route of rail lines between the two cities. These rails can belong to different players. - Closest meants you must use the fewest number of rail lines possible (lines, not individual train chips). In the event of a tie, you use the route with the most train chips - Award points for the route - For each rail line along the route, its owner gets 1 point. - If the destination station has a player's station marker on it, that player gets 1 point - Take the goods toekn from the producer city and put it face down on the station, covering the goods request icon. This means each request can only be met once each game.

docs.title: Spending Money
docs.text:
With this action, you buy new chips from the market. Some of the chips in your boarding area will generate money, this is the total you can spend this turn. If you trash a chip, you still get to use any money it generated this turn. Any money not spent on a turn is lost.

A chip's cost is determined by the compartment that it is in. When you buy a chip, it goes to your warehouse to get put in your bag later. Chips are immediately replenished

  • Chips have notches on them to show the initial cost. Place the new chip in the leftmost empty space of that compartment. If the slots in that compartment are already full, create an empty space by sliding chips there one slot to the left. If you are unable to do so, move chips further down the market to the left, including moving the very first chip off of the board and into the trash.

In addition to the chips in the market, there are some other ways printed on the board to spend money:

  • For $2, you may buy one of the $1+ chips placed in setup if there are any remaining
  • for $4, if the lighting train market toek has been moved here already (from the scoring track), you may take a lightning train
  • For $7 you may take a lightning train and score 1 point
  • For $8, you may take two lightning trains and score 1 point

You can also take a loan to increase how much money you have to spend on a turn

  • Take a loan chip from the bank and put it in your warehouse, you now have $1 more to spend this turn. You can only do this once per turn.
  • Paying off a loan requires you to pay $4 on a later turn when the chip is in your boarding area

docs.title: Lightning trains
docs.text:
We've talked about getting lightning trains a lot of different ways. Just to recap, whenever you get the lightning train symbol from anywhere, you just take a train chip from your supply and put it in the lightning area of your boarding area to use this turn. You also get one from:

  • Every 2 conductor icons (reminder on top left of playerboard)
  • If you went public and get a delivery symbol ( just get the symbol, you don't have to make a delivery)

docs.title: Turnover phase
docs.text: Once your turn is done, any chips left in your boarding area go to your warehouse. If you used a starting contract in your boarding area to do an action this turn, it is trashed instead. Draw new chips to fill your boarding area, and the next player can trake their turn

docs.title: Transcontinental Railroad
docs.text:
The moment there is an unbroken route of rail lines connecting New York and San fransico, we pause to do a scoring.

  • Determine the shortest route as if you were making a delivery
  • For each train chip on that route, its owner scores 2 points
  • No player can build stations anymore

docs.title: Game end
docs.text:
Game end is trigered if the transcontinental railroad has been established, and specific cities are connected to it

  • Less than four players either Houston, Seattle, or both
  • 4 players both Houston and Seattle

Finish out the round that this happens in, and then play 1 more full round. After the end of the final round, in turn order all players will make one last delivery as if they had a delivery icon

  • Each player loses 2 points for each loan chip they have anywhere
  • Each player reveals and scores all of their goal cards

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: In 1755 Lisboa (thats in Portugal) suffered an earthquake of 8.5-9 magnitude, followed by an earthquake, and then 3 days of fires. The city was almost completely destroyed. The game Lisboa is set over 22 years in which the surviving nobles (thats us) will help with the reconstruction and economic development of the new city to receive the graces of the king and the marquis.

docs.title: Board Overview
docs.text: We'll need to clear rubble and build stores downtown (gesture towards grid where buildings will be built), we'll need to work with the architects to get plans for public buildings to build ( gesture at the blue and green architect piles) and make sure we have enough officials to staff the buildings. We'll need influence (gesture to influence track) to convince the nobles (gesture at the nobles) to help us, or we can also trade with them for state actions. We can meet with the clergy (gesture towards area with red meeple) to get clergy tiles which grant permanent bonuses, and we can also persuade the Marquis to make decrees that favor us, giving us more points at the end of the game (gesture at decree card lineup on the side of the board)

docs.title: Playerboard overview
docs.text:
• This is your portfolio (gesture at playerboard). Your warehouse on the left is where you hold your resources, and you start the game with one of each.
  • You then have a section where you store the rubble you collect in the game. The rubble cooresponds to the type of destruction that occured, red for fire damage, blue for tsunami damage, and tan for earthquake damage.
  • Collecting sets of rubble with both move along the pace of the game and dictate how many total resources you can hold as well as how many total cards you can play to your board.
  • Currently we can store 2 of each type of resource, and have a total of 2 cards played to our boards.
  • Comleting a full set of rubble increases the capacity of each by 1
  • In terms of playing cards to your board you have 3 wide slots up top, and 3 smaller slots at the bottom.
  • Top part of the board is where noble cards and ships go, the bottom part of the board is where treasury cards go.
  • To reiterate, your card capacity number refers to total cards in the top and bottom of your board.
  • You also have a space to store your royal favors (under the rubble track)
  • A space to store your building plans (to the right of favors), and we all start with one.
  • Your buildings
  • Your state officials (meeples)
  • Your clergy tiles (top right). We all start with the choice of one of two dealt during setup.

docs.title: Game Structure
docs.text: The game is played over 2 time periods. When a player manages to complete 2 sets of ruble or 3 decks of political cards are exhausted, the round ends at the end of that players turn. There will then be a mid game scoring. We'll then swap some stuff out on the board and start the 2nd time period until the end of the game is triggered by someone having 4 complete sets of rubble, or 3 decks of cards being exhuasted again. We will finish out the round, and then everyone wil lget one more turn before doing end game scoring

docs.title: Turn Structure
docs.text:
You will always have a hand of 5 political cards. Your turn consists of you playing a card in your hand to perform one of 4 actions or to get 1 gold. Then you will take a new card into your hand from the display and it will be the next players turn. The turn structure is detailed on the back of your player aid.

There are only 2 types of political cards. Nobles and treasury cards.

docs.title: Noble Cards
docs.text:
• Each noble card depicts one of the 3 nobles
  • There is an amount of influence at the top of the card.
  • The picture of the noble and flavor text in the middle
  • A reward or penalty at the bottom of the card

docs.title: Treasury Cards
docs.text:
• Subsidy and Treasury modifyer up top
  • Action or reward in the middle
  • Flavor text
  • Money or Sales benefit

docs.title: Playing a card
docs.text:
• On your turn you will select a card from your hand and decide whether to play it to your portfolio, or play it to the royal court to visit the nobles on the main board.
  • Your turn structure is detailed on the back of your player aid as well as the section in the middle of your board.

docs.title: Playing card to portfolio
docs.text:
• (Point to the top part of the left half of the turn summary on the playerboard)
  • Decide to play a noble card to the top of your board, or a treasury card to the bottom
  • Playing a noble card to the top earns you the bonus shown on the arrow at the bottom of the card, then you tuck that card in the top of your playerboard.
  • Treasury cards are tucked in the bottom and earn you money equal to the current treasury value (point to the big cube on main board), and then decrease the treasury marker
  • Treasury also have an ongoing effect shown at the bottom that remains visible when tucking the card.

You then decide whether to sell goods or trade with a noble (point to the bottom part of the left half of the turn summary on the playerboard)

docs.title: Selling a good (main action)
docs.text:
• To sell a good, someone at the table must have a ship built on the top part of their portfolio (grab a ship card and put it in the top of the portfolio to show)
  • If there is a ship built and it has room for goods (point to the box and number on the ship card) you can sell to it.
  • Select any docked ship with space and move a good from your warehouse under that ship into one of the circular spaces. This could mean that you are putting a good from your portfolio onto another players portfolio.
  • Recieve money equal to the goods current price (gesture towards good price track), plus any modifier depicted on the ship.

If the number of goods on the dock spaces below the ship are now equal to the ship size, the ship immediately sets sail and the owner of the ship completes these steps

  • Flip the goods tokens on the dock spaces below the ship face down so the crate side shows
  • Earn 1 point per good packed up

You can sell any number of goods to any number of ships up to their capacity.

Your other choice instead of selling a good is to trade with a noble (point at handshake symbol on left half of turn reminder on player board)

docs.title: Trading with a noble (main action)
docs.text:
This action allows you to take up to two different state actions that are associated with each noble on the main board. The state actions are shown in the little banners under each noble's portrait.

  • Each noble is associated with 2 specific state actions, and each noble cares about one type of good.
  • Trading with a noble allows you to take up to 2 actions, but each action will cost you a good.
  • In the banner showing the actions, you can also see the good required to trade with that noble. You can also use gold with each noble instead.
  • When you take an action, take the good or money from your portfolio and cover up the action on the main board in that noble's section.
  • One that action is complete, you decide if you want to pay another resource to take the other available action under that noble.
  • First action is mandatory, second is optional.

docs.title: State actions
docs.text:
We will start with the builder's office (the green guy)

Recruiting state officials (two men symbol)

  • Move up to two state officials, those are your meeples, from your playerboard to two different nobles offices.
  • If the office has no free space for your official, determine which color has the most meeples in that office and move one to the plaza below to free up space for your meeple
  • In the event of a tie, move one of each tied color

Acquire a plan (the paper)

  • Take the top plan from either architects stack (gesture to the blue and green builders on the right side of the board)
  • Place it on the proper space on your player board, architect side up.
  • You can have any number of plans from both architects

Next the Marquis office (the blue guy)

Build a ship (the boat)

  • Pay a number of different goods equal to the hull size of the ship visible in the shipyard (point to the ship cards on the main board)
  • You pay the difference in size if you are replacing a ship in your portfolio
  • If you replace a ship, the cargo stays where it was (it is loaded on to the new ship)
  • Select an empty slot at the top of your board or discard a card to make room for the ship
  • Move the tresury marker up one spce
  • Earn the influence shown at the top of all your tucked cards, including the new ship (point at the influence track) More on the influence track later

Produce goods (the gears)

  • From each store you own, take one good of its business type (the street that it is facing) from the supply. More on that in a bit
  • Reminder that your limit of goods you can hold is 2 of each plus 1 additional for each rubble set you've completed
  • Each type of good produced drops 1 money in price, so the market marker for it moves accordingly (point at goods market price track)

Finally the Kings office (the pink one)

Meet the cardinal (cross with arrows)

  • Advance the cardinal meeple clockwise one or two spaces. The spaces he moves are in between the clergy tiles.
  • Take either one of the tiles now adjacent to the cardinal, placing it face up on your player board.
  • You must have free space for this. Once you have 4 tiles, you cannot take this action.
  • Check to see if the cardinal landed on or passed over an icon.
  • Depending on symobs the cardinal has moved over, either increase the treasure marker 1 space, or perform a church scoring at the end of that players turn
  • For church socring each player, starting with the player who moved the cardinal, decides whether to gain influence or not.
  • To gain influence, discard a clergy tile. Earn the points shown on the back. Gain influence shown on your tucked cards.

Get a royal favor (the crown)

  • Each royal favor tile depicts on of the nobles and allows you to take an extra action in that nobles office during any other players turn
  • You can only get a royal favor that you do not already own.
  • Grab a tile and put it in the appropriate spot on your board.

TO REITERATE, PLAYING A CARD TO YOUR BOARD ALLOWS YOU TO THEN EITHER SELL GOODS TO SHIPS, OR TAKE STATE ACTIONS WITH A SINGLE NOBLE

EACH OF THESE OPTIONS REQUIRES YOU TO PAY GOODS FROM YOUR STOREHOUSE, SO IF YOU DON'T HAVE GOODS (OR MONEY FOR NOBLES) THEN YOU CAN'T DO THESE OPTIONS

docs.title: Playing card to the royal court
docs.text:
